does: This survey provides a comprehensive examination of alignment techniques for large language models, categorizing methods into outer alignment (training and fine-tuning approaches) and inner alignment (understanding and controlling internal model behavior). It systematically reviews current alignment methodologies designed to ensure LLMs produce outputs consistent with human values rather than misleading or harmful content. The work also addresses critical cross-cutting issues including model interpretability, adversarial vulnerabilities, and the technical challenges of verifying alignment. Beyond cataloging existing approaches, the survey synthesizes evaluation benchmarks and methodologies used to assess whether alignment techniques actually work, establishing a structured framework for understanding the current state of LLM safety research.
connects: This work builds on decades of AI alignment research that has sought to ensure advanced systems behave according to human intent, adapting classical alignment problems to the specific challenges posed by large language models. It integrates insights from reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), constitutional AI, mechanistic interpretability, and adversarial robustness research. The survey connects to earlier work on value learning, specification gaming, and goal misalignment while addressing LLM-specific concerns about hallucination, jailbreaking, and scalable oversight. It extends prior surveys on language model safety and builds on the growing interdisciplinary literature examining how large neural networks can be steered toward beneficial behaviors through training modifications and architectural constraints.
enables: This comprehensive synthesis provides a foundation for advancing LLM safety by identifying gaps between current alignment techniques and the actual safety guarantees needed for deployment in high-stakes settings. It enables researchers to design more sophisticated alignment methods by clarifying which approaches address outer versus inner alignment challenges. The survey points toward development of better interpretability tools, more robust evaluation metrics that capture subtle alignment failures, and integration of multiple alignment approaches rather than relying on single techniques. It motivates research into formal verification of alignment properties, more sophisticated adversarial testing frameworks, and scalable oversight mechanisms. Future work can build on this taxonomic foundation to develop provably robust alignment techniques and better understand the fundamental limits of aligning increasingly capable models.
